annotations.label: Simple continued fraction

anchor.snippet: A simple or regular continued fraction is a continued fraction with numerators all equal to one

annotations.note: `SimpContFract` is defined as a `GenContFract` whose partial numerators are all 1.

annotations.label: Coefficients/terms of a continued fraction

anchor.snippet: are called the coefficients or terms of the continued fraction

annotations.note: `partNums`/`partDens` and the head term `h` formalize the coefficients of a (generalized) continued fraction.

annotations.label: Rational has two finite continued fraction expressions

anchor.snippet: has two closely related expressions as a finite continued fraction

annotations.note: Termination ↔ rationality is formalized, but the dual-representation (`[…;a]` vs `[…;a−1,1]`) uniqueness fact for rationals is not.

annotations.label: Irrationality of infinite continued fraction value

anchor.snippet: The numerical value of an infinite continued fraction is irrational

annotations.note: The contrapositive for the canonical expansion `of v` is captured by `terminates_iff_rat`, but no statement covers value-of-an-arbitrary-infinite-SCF being irrational.

annotations.label: Unique infinite continued fraction for irrationals

anchor.snippet: every irrational number

annotations.note: The canonical expansion `GenContFract.of v` is constructed and shown to converge to `v`, but uniqueness among all infinite SCFs is not formalized.

annotations.label: Continued fraction of 415/93

anchor.snippet: Consider, for example, the rational number

annotations.note: No specific numeric example of 415/93's continued fraction in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bracket notation for continued fractions

anchor.snippet: This can be represented by the abbreviated notation

annotations.note: Bracket notation `[a₀; a₁, a₂, …]` is mentioned only informally in docstrings; no Lean notation is registered.

annotations.label: Continued fractions of √19, e, π, φ, γ

anchor.snippet: Examples of continued fraction representations of irrational numbers are

annotations.note: No closed-form CF expansions for √19, e, π, φ, or γ are formalized.

annotations.label: Finite iff rational

anchor.snippet: The continued fraction representation for a real number is finite if and only if it is a rational number

annotations.note: `(of v).Terminates ↔ ∃ q : ℚ, v = q` is `terminates_iff_rat`.

annotations.label: Two representations of a rational

anchor.snippet: Each rational can be represented in exactly two ways

annotations.note: The fact that every rational has exactly two finite SCF representations (related by splitting the last term) is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Uniqueness for irrationals

anchor.snippet: The simple continued fraction representation of an irrational number is unique

annotations.note: No uniqueness statement for the SCF expansion of an irrational is present.

annotations.label: Eventually periodic iff quadratic irrational

anchor.snippet: The real numbers whose continued fraction eventually repeats are precisely the quadratic irrationals

annotations.note: Lagrange's periodicity theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Canonical form of a continued fraction

anchor.snippet: A continued fraction in canonical form is an expression of the form

annotations.note: `ContFract` = SCF with positive partial denominators is the canonical (regular) form.

annotations.label: Finite, infinite, periodic continued fraction

anchor.snippet: When the expression contains finitely many terms, it is called a finite continued fraction

annotations.note: `Terminates`/`TerminatedAt` formalize finiteness vs. infiniteness; periodicity is not defined.

annotations.label: Recursive definition of terms

anchor.snippet: the

annotations.note: `contsAux` gives the recursive computation of the numerator/denominator continuants.

annotations.label: Limit definition of infinite simple continued fraction

anchor.snippet: One may also define infinite simple continued fractions as limits

annotations.note: `of_convergence` shows convergents of `of v` tend to `v`, providing the limit definition.

annotations.label: Recursive definition of continued fraction representation

anchor.snippet: Consider a real number

annotations.note: `IntFractPair.stream` recursively builds ⟨⌊v⌋, fract v⟩ then ⌊(fract v)⁻¹⌋, … exactly as in the article.

annotations.label: Algorithm for continued fraction representation

anchor.snippet: In order to calculate a continued fraction representation

annotations.note: `GenContFract.of` implements the floor/reciprocal algorithm; its correctness is `of_correctness_of_terminatedAt` and `of_convergence`.

annotations.label: Reciprocal shift property

anchor.snippet: The continued fraction representations of a positive rational number and its reciprocal are identical except for a shift one place left or right

annotations.note: No general reciprocal-shift lemma for `GenContFract.of` is present.

annotations.label: Reciprocal with integer leading term

anchor.snippet: For instance if

annotations.note: A specific reciprocal example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Last term equal for number and reciprocal

anchor.snippet: The last number that generates the remainder of the continued fraction is the same for both

annotations.note: No statement about the last term being equal under reciprocation.

annotations.label: Finite continued fractions represent rationals (two ways)

anchor.snippet: Every finite continued fraction represents a rational number

annotations.note: Termination ↔ rationality is formalized, but the article's stronger claim that every finite SCF evaluates to a rational (and dually) is not stated for arbitrary SCFs.

annotations.label: Infinite continued fraction iff irrational

anchor.snippet: Every infinite continued fraction is irrational

annotations.note: Captured for the canonical `of v`; no theorem about arbitrary infinite SCFs evaluating to irrationals.

annotations.label: Convergents

anchor.snippet: These rational numbers are called the convergents of the continued fraction

annotations.note: `convs n = nums n / dens n` is the convergent sequence.

annotations.label: Gaussian bracket recurrence for convergents

anchor.snippet: the relevant recursive relation is that of Gaussian brackets

annotations.note: `conts_recurrence`, `nums_recurrence`, `dens_recurrence` give Aₙ = bₙAₙ₋₁ + aₙAₙ₋₂ etc.

annotations.label: Even/odd convergents bound the value

anchor.snippet: Even-numbered convergents are smaller than the original number, while odd-numbered ones are larger

annotations.note: Parity-based bounding of convergents around `v` is not stated.

annotations.label: Babylonian method approximants appear as convergents

anchor.snippet: When using the Babylonian method to generate successive approximations

annotations.note: Babylonian method/CF link not formalized.

annotations.label: Baire space homeomorphism

anchor.snippet: The infinite continued fraction provides a homeomorphism from the Baire space to the space of irrational real numbers

annotations.note: No CF-Baire-space homeomorphism in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Minkowski question-mark mapping

anchor.snippet: this map is called the Minkowski question-mark function

annotations.note: Minkowski's `?` function is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauss–Kuzmin distribution

anchor.snippet: is the Gauss–Kuzmin distribution

annotations.note: Gauss–Kuzmin distribution is not formalized.

anchor.snippet: Corollary 1: Each convergent is in its lowest terms

annotations.note: Determinant formula gives coprimality, but `gcd(Aₙ,Bₙ)=1` is not stated as a separate Mathlib lemma.

anchor.snippet: Corollary 2: The difference between successive convergents is a fraction whose numerator is unity

annotations.note: Equivalent rearrangement of the determinant formula.

annotations.note: No alternating-series identity for CFs in Mathlib.
